title: After Hours
tonic_progression: C#m (Aeolian, picking up exactly where Vol. 1 closed — the platform, but the gate is shut: the last train is gone) -> F#m (Aeolian, the locked station concourse) -> Bm (Dorian, back out into the soaked back-streets) -> Em (Aeolian, the all-night convenience-store counter) -> Am (Dorian, the underground arcade still humming at 3am) -> Dm (Aeolian, the standing ramen counter, steam and stillness) -> Gm (Dorian, the river bridge in the deepest hour) -> Cm (Aeolian, the capsule-hotel corridor, no vacancy) -> Fm (Phrygian, the lowest hour, the city at its most empty and strange) -> A#m / Bbm (Aeolian, the sky going from black to slate, the first delivery trucks) -> D#m (Dorian, the platform again as the gate unlocks, grey-blue pre-dawn) -> C#m (Aeolian resolving to the Picardy-tinted relative warmth at first light — the first train, the platform motif resolved, home at last)
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks carry the listener through a single stranded night — the inverse of Vol. 1's motion. Where Vol. 1 was a salaryman racing the clock to catch the last train, Vol. 2 opens at the moment after: he MISSED it. The gate is shut, the platform empty, and there is nothing to do now but wait out the hours until first service. This is an album of STILLNESS AND WANDERING, not the chase — pedestrian throughout, never crossing 92 BPM, the city slowed to a soaked, sleepless drift. Track 1 picks up exactly where Vol. 1's 'The Long Way Home' left off (C# minor, the empty platform, rain), but the warmth of going home is gone — the LAST-TRAIN PULSE (Vol. 1's countdown clatter) is heard one last time receding into the tunnel and then SILENCED, the countdown over, the train gone. Tracks 1-3 are the stranding and the first wander: the shut gate (1, C#m), the locked concourse where the figure realizes he is here until dawn (2, F#m), and the step back out into the rain-soaked Shinjuku back-streets to wait it out (3, Bm). Tracks 4-6 are the all-night counters, the small lit refuges that keep a stranded city alive: the fluorescent convenience-store at the corner (4, Em), the underground arcade still glowing and humming at 3am (5, Am — the PLATFORM CHIME re-voiced, drowned and warped, bleeding through the cabinet noise), and the standing ramen counter where steam and a single bowl mark the deepest stillness (6, Dm — the SALARYMAN LAMENT re-voiced over a Rhodes, weary and alone but no longer rushing). Tracks 7-9 are the deepest hour, the wander at its loneliest and strangest: the river bridge where the city's reflection trembles in black water (7, Gm), the capsule-hotel corridor turned away at the desk, no vacancy (8, Cm), and the lowest hour of all — 4am, the Phrygian floor, the city at its emptiest and most uncanny, neither night nor morning (9, Fm). Tracks 10-12 are the turn toward dawn: the sky shifting from black to slate as the first delivery trucks rumble through and the rain finally thins (10, Bbm), the return to the station as the gate is unlocked into grey-blue pre-dawn (11, D#m — the LAST-TRAIN PULSE returns, but now as the FIRST-train pulse, the countdown reborn as a beginning), and the closer at first light: the first train arrives, the platform held all night now bright and clean, and the RAIN CELL and PLATFORM CHIME and SALARYMAN LAMENT all resolve together — the platform motif that haunted Vol. 1 finally answered, the long stranded night ending not in defeat but in the quiet relief of going home at dawn (12, C#m, resolving with a Picardy-tinted warmth Vol. 1 never allowed itself — the album's only moment of true light, earned by surviving the whole night). The ring is the same C# minor that opened and closed Vol. 1, but where Vol. 1 ended on stillness unresolved, Vol. 2 ends on stillness RESOLVED — the rain washed away, the first light clean, the platform motif at rest.

recurring_motifs.motif: RAIN CELL (carried from Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 leitmotif #1) — the four-note descending detuned-FM-bell figure (b6-5-4-3) that was Vol. 1's weather signature, doubled by tape-saturated rain foley. Here it is RE-VOICED for the stranded stillness of the after-hours: slower, wetter, more spaced-out, the same falling contour but held longer between notes (the night has no hurry now). It is the album's weather thread — the rain that strands the figure at track 1, soaks the whole wander, finally thins toward dawn (track 10), and is washed clean away at first light (track 12, where it returns whole and DRY one last time, the storm over).
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 states it slow and wet on FM bell as the rain seals the figure on the empty platform (the same dry-then-wet contour Vol. 1 opened on, but slowed for the stillness). Track 3 spreads it across the soaked back-streets, doubled and detuned into the puddles. Track 7 stretches it to half-time pads over the black river, the rain at its most ambient and reflective. Track 10 THINS it — the figure heard fewer and fewer notes as the rain finally eases toward dawn (the storm breaking). Track 12 returns it WHOLE AND DRY one last time at first light, the rain over, the contour clean and undetuned for the first time in two albums — the weather signature finally at rest.

recurring_motifs.motif: LAST-TRAIN PULSE -> FIRST-TRAIN PULSE (carried from Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 leitmotif #2, TRANSFORMED) — Vol. 1's muted sidechained 16th-note square-wave arpeggio that evoked rail clatter and a countdown clock toward the last train. In Vol. 2 its meaning INVERTS across the album: it opens as the last-train pulse RECEDING and then SILENCED (the train the figure missed pulling away down the tunnel), is absent through the long stranded hours (there is no train to catch — the pulse has nothing to count down), and RETURNS at the pre-dawn as the FIRST-train pulse, the same clatter reborn as a countdown toward a BEGINNING rather than a deadline. The album's structural spine: the pulse that meant 'you are about to be stranded' in Vol. 1 becomes the pulse that means 'you are about to go home' in Vol. 2.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 plays it ONCE, distant and receding down the tunnel, then SILENCES it — the last train pulling away, the countdown over, the figure left on the platform (the inverse of Vol. 1, where the pulse drove him toward the train; here it leaves without him). It is then deliberately ABSENT through tracks 2-10 (there is no train to catch all night — the pulse has nothing to count). Track 11 RETURNS it as the FIRST-train pulse approaching from far down the line as the gate unlocks into pre-dawn — the same square-wave clatter, but warming and brightening as it nears rather than receding, a countdown toward a beginning. Track 12 brings it to FULL and CLEAN at first light as the first train arrives — the clatter no longer muted or anxious but steady and warm, the pulse that stranded him now the pulse that carries him home, the countdown clock turned into a clock counting UP into morning.

recurring_motifs.motif: SALARYMAN LAMENT (carried from Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 leitmotif #3) — the weary five-note vocal-formant lead phrased like a sigh, always landing on the minor third, that was Vol. 1's human voice. Here it is re-voiced for exhaustion-without-urgency: where Vol. 1's lament was the sigh of a man rushing and missing, Vol. 2's is the slower sigh of a man with nowhere to be and all night to feel it — the same five-note shape, but unhurried, spaced, almost resigned, gradually softening from weariness (track 6) toward acceptance and finally a faint warmth (track 12).
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 6 states it solo over a Rhodes at the standing ramen counter — the same weary five-note sigh as Vol. 1, but slowed and unhurried, a man eating alone at 3am with nowhere to be. Track 9 plays it at its loneliest and most fragmented at the lowest hour, the phrase breaking apart over the Phrygian floor, the sigh of the emptiest moment of the night. Track 12 plays it WHOLE one last time at first light — but for the first time it does NOT land on the minor third: the final note lifts to the major third (a Picardy turn), the sigh resolved into the faint relief of going home, the lament finally answered. This is the resolution Vol. 1 never gave it (Vol. 1's lament walked the last block home unaccompanied and unresolved; Vol. 2's reaches the morning).

recurring_motifs.motif: PLATFORM CHIME (carried from Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 leitmotif #4) — the two-note major-second departure-jingle deliberately too bright for the key, the station announcement bleeding through. In Vol. 1 it was stated at true pitch in the arcade, pitched down a tritone into menace at the closing doors, and restored to true pitch as the very last sound. In Vol. 2 it is the album's RESOLUTION THREAD: it returns warped and drowned through the long night (a jingle for a train that won't come), and is RESTORED to true pitch — and finally made to feel EARNED rather than ironic — at first light when it announces the first train. This is the 'platform motif returns resolved at first light' the album is built around.
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 5 re-introduces it in the underground arcade, WARPED — pitched-bent and drowned in reverb and cabinet-noise, the cheerful jingle turned uncanny and wrong at 3am, a departure announcement for a station with no departures. Track 11 plays it HALF-RESTORED at the pre-dawn gate — pulled back toward true pitch as the first service nears, still slightly reverbed but no longer warped, the jingle remembering what it is for. Track 12 RESTORES it fully to true pitch at first light as the genuine first-train announcement — and for the first time across two albums it sounds EARNED rather than too-bright-and-ironic, because now there really is a train, and a way home; the platform motif that haunted both albums finally resolved, bright and right, into the morning.

tracks.title: Missed It by a Minute

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: deep sub drone on C#, the RAIN CELL slow and wet on detuned FM bell, tape-saturated rain foley, the shutter-clack of a closing station gate, distant fluorescent hum, no rhythm — picking up exactly where Vol. 1 'The Long Way Home' left the platform

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + the LAST-TRAIN PULSE heard ONCE, distant and receding down the tunnel, then SILENCED (the train pulling away without him), soft sub bass on the root, a single very soft kick on beat 1

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + wide PWM pad settling into resignation, the rain cell holding, brushed gated snare on beat 4 only, mellow saw counter-line low in the mix, the deflated stillness of being left behind

sections.name: bridge
sections.instrumentation: drums drop, rain cell alone over the pad, the empty platform, the realization that there is nothing now but the wait

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: pad sustains C#, rain cell once more, filter close leaning toward F# minor — the figure turns to leave the platform for the long night, fade to rain

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo 80 BPM C# minor, slow wet detuned FM bell melody descending four-note figure spaced wide, deep sub bass, tape-saturated rain ambience, distant station gate clatter receding into a tunnel then silence, fluorescent hum, very soft single kick, brushed gated snare on beat four only, wide PWM pad, the deflated stillness of just missing the last train and being stranded for the night, Akira meets Blade Runner, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, a lone silhouette in a long coat standing alone at a shut station gate as the tail-lights of the last train recede into a tunnel, empty rain-wet platform, magenta and cyan signage reflected on the wet floor, a closed shutter half-down, deflated lonely after-hours mood, anamorphic cinematic, deep shadow, no warm sunset, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.composer_notes: OPENER / VOL. 1 SEAM PICKUP. Sets the tonic C# minor — the SAME home tonic that opened and closed Vol. 1 ('Last Whistle, Floor 31' and 'The Long Way Home'), but where Vol. 1 began on the descent into the night and ended walking home, Vol. 2 begins on the moment AFTER the last train is missed: the gate shut, the platform empty, the whole night ahead. Re-states the RAIN CELL slow and wet (Vol. 1's weather signature, slowed for stillness) and plays the LAST-TRAIN PULSE ONCE receding then SILENCED — the inverse of Vol. 1, where the pulse drove the chase; here the train leaves without him. This is the slowest, stillest opener at 80 BPM (the pedestrian floor). No four-on-the-floor ever in this album — it is wandering, not motion. Outro leans toward F# minor of track 2 (the figure leaving the platform for the locked concourse).

tracks.title: Locked Concourse

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: pad in F# minor in a vast echoing concourse reverb, soft kick on 1, the distant drip of rain through a vent, footstep echo, no melody yet

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a slow lonely plucked arpeggio echoing down the empty hall, warm analog bass, gated snare on 2 and 4 soft, the long fluorescent corridors with all the shops shuttered

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + a thin PWM lead on a slow four-bar phrase, layered cold pads, soft sidechain almost off, the realization settling in: stranded here until first service

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: lead drops, the plucked arpeggio echoes alone, a second voice an octave up, the figure walking the length of the empty concourse

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: full but quiet mix, lead doubled with a soft FM bell counter-line echoing

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on C# (V of F#m), pad sustains in the echo — the figure pushes out through the side door into the rain, the back-streets next

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo synthwave 84 BPM F# minor, vast echoing empty station concourse reverb, a slow lonely plucked arpeggio echoing down a long fluorescent hall, warm analog bass, soft brushed gated drums, thin PWM lead on a slow phrase, layered cold pads, footstep echo and distant rain drip, the resignation of being locked in a closed station until dawn, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, a vast empty station concourse at night with all the shops shuttered behind rolling steel grilles, long rows of cold fluorescent light receding to a vanishing point, a lone tiny silhouette walking the length of the empty hall, faint magenta and cyan glow from a shuttered kiosk, echoing emptiness, cinematic anamorphic, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from C#m to F#m, the same cycle-of-fifths-inside-the-minor-mode language Vol. 1 used for its opening ascent — but here it climbs into stillness rather than motion, the wander rather than the chase.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM, the same gentle Vol. 1 increment, but the energy stays inward; this is a man walking slowly through an empty hall, not accelerating into a night.
tracks.composer_notes: The stranding settles into resignation. Honors Vol. 1's +4-BPM cycle-of-fifths language (Vol. 1 went C#m -> G#m up a fifth; Vol. 2 goes C#m -> F#m up a fifth differently to signal a new, slower path). Keep it spacious and echoing — the concourse reverb is the character of the track. The LAST-TRAIN PULSE stays absent from here on (there is no train to catch). Outro C# (V of F#m) pivots to Bm of track 3 (out into the back-streets).

tracks.title: Back-Streets, No Umbrella

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: pad in B minor Dorian (raised 6th, G# natural, keeping the wander warm rather than mournful), soft kick on 1 and 3, the RAIN CELL spreading across the soaked back-streets doubled and detuned into the puddles, neon-buzz ambience

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a mid-tempo plucked arpeggio, warm walking bass, gated snare on 2 and 4, the rain cell doubled into the puddle reflections, the slow aimless wander through narrow neon alleys

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + a mellow saw lead on a wandering phrase, layered pads, soft sidechain, the Dorian G# keeping it bittersweet rather than grim, magenta and cyan signs smearing in the wet

sections.name: breakdown
sections.instrumentation: drums drop, the rain cell and a single sustained pad, a puddle-reflection of a neon sign rippling

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: drums return, lead doubled with an FM bell counter, the wander at its most open

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on F# (V of Bm), pad sustains, rain foley — a lit convenience-store sign glows at the end of the alley

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo synthwave 88 BPM B minor Dorian with raised sixth G natural, a mid-tempo plucked arpeggio, a slow wet detuned FM bell rain figure doubled into puddle reflections, warm walking bass, soft gated drums, a mellow wandering saw lead, layered pads, neon-buzz ambience, the aimless soaked drift through narrow magenta-and-cyan back-streets with nowhere to be, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, a lone figure wandering down a narrow rain-soaked Shinjuku back-alley at night with no umbrella, dense magenta and cyan signage crowding overhead, neon reflections rippling in puddles underfoot, steam from a vent, aimless lonely drift, cinematic anamorphic shallow focus, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fourth from F#m to Bm, voiced Dorian (raised 6th) to keep the wander bittersweet rather than mournful — the harmony eases outward into the streets after the echoing interior of the concourse.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM, the gentle increment continuing; this is the most 'moving' the album gets for a while, but it is a walk, not a run — well under the pedestrian cap.
tracks.composer_notes: The first true wander. RAIN CELL spread across the back-streets, doubled into the puddles. Dorian raised-6th (G# natural) is the album's signal for the bittersweet-warm rather than grim color — keep it wistful, not desolate. Outro F# (V of Bm) pivots to Em of track 4 (the convenience-store counter).

tracks.title: Konbini, 2 a.m.

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: warm Rhodes chords in E minor, soft kick on 1 and 3, the gentle door-chime and cooler-hum of an all-night convenience store, bright fluorescent ambience

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a soft plucked arpeggio like the warm interior light, warm sub bass, brushed gated snare, the small comfort of a lit refuge from the rain

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + a gentle mellow saw lead, layered warm pads, soft sidechain almost off, the bittersweet coziness of a bright empty store at 2am, a hot can of coffee from the warmer

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: lead drops, Rhodes center, a soft FM bell shadow, the figure standing at the magazine rack out of the rain

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: full but soft mix, lead octave-doubled gently

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on B (V of Em), Rhodes sustains, the door-chime as the figure steps back out — the arcade glow next

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo synthwave 90 BPM E minor, warm mellow Rhodes chords, a soft plucked arpeggio like warm interior light, warm sub bass, soft brushed gated drums, a gentle mellow saw lead, layered warm pads, the door-chime and cooler-hum of an all-night convenience store, bright fluorescent ambience, the small bittersweet comfort of a lit refuge from the rain at 2am, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, the warm bright interior of an all-night Japanese convenience store seen from the rainy street outside through a glass front, glowing white-and-warm fluorescent light spilling onto wet black pavement, a lone figure inside at the counter, magenta and cyan neon reflected in the wet street, small warm refuge against the cold night, cinematic, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fourth from Bm to Em, the natural minor giving a softer, warmer interior color after the Dorian streets — the harmonic equivalent of stepping out of the rain into warm light.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM, a small lift but the energy turns inward and warm; the only small refuge of the early hours, a held breath of comfort.
tracks.composer_notes: BRIDGE / first warm refuge. The album's first genuinely warm track — a Rhodes-led pause, the small human comfort of an all-night store. Keeps the audience-appropriate coziness (2am coders will recognize the konbini). Sidechain almost off — warm, not pumping. Outro B (V of Em) pivots to Am of track 5 (down into the arcade).

tracks.title: Arcade Still Open

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: pad in A minor Dorian (raised 6th, F# natural), soft kick on 1 and 3, the layered bleeps and attract-mode loops of an arcade still running at 3am, glitch-percussion textures

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a square-wave arpeggio half-buried in the cabinet noise, warm bass, gated snare, the WARPED PLATFORM CHIME re-introduced pitch-bent and drowned in reverb, bleeding uncannily through the machines

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + a mellow PWM lead, the Dorian F# lending an odd brightness, layered pads, soft sidechain, the too-awake glow of a basement arcade nobody else is in

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: drums drop, the warped chime and the arp alone, a cabinet's attract-loop flickering, the uncanny stillness inside all the noise

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: full return, lead doubled, the warped chime articulated twice more, drowned and strange

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on E (V of Am), pad sustains, the bleeps fade as the figure climbs back to the street — the ramen counter next

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo synthwave 92 BPM A minor Dorian with raised sixth F natural, a square-wave arpeggio half-buried in arcade cabinet noise, glitchy 8-bit-tinged percussion, a pitch-bent two-note chime drowned in reverb bleeding uncannily through the machines, warm bass, soft gated drums, a mellow PWM lead with an odd brightness, layered pads, the too-awake insomniac glow of a basement arcade still running at 3am with nobody in it, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, a narrow basement arcade corridor still lit at 3am, rows of glowing game cabinets in cyan and magenta running attract-mode loops to an empty room, reflective wet tile floor, a single lone figure standing among the machines, uncanny overbright insomniac mood, cinematic, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Em to Am, voiced Dorian (raised 6th, F# natural) for the uncanny too-bright color — the harmonic mark of the after-hours arcade, a place too awake for the hour.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM, the album's tempo ceiling reached here (92) — still firmly pedestrian; this is the most 'lit' and busy the night gets, all noise and no motion.
tracks.composer_notes: Re-introduces the PLATFORM CHIME (Vol. 1's motif #4) WARPED — pitch-bent and drowned, the cheerful jingle turned uncanny, a departure announcement for a station with no departures (it will be half-restored in track 11 and fully resolved at first light in track 12). This is the album's BPM ceiling at 92 — deliberately well under the pedestrian cap; the arcade is busy with noise, not speed. Outro E (V of Am) pivots to Dm of track 6 (the ramen counter).

tracks.title: One Bowl, Steam Rising

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: warm Rhodes chords in D minor, soft kick on 1, the low hum of a tiny standing ramen counter, the hiss of a pot, amber pendant-lamp warmth

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + the SALARYMAN LAMENT solo on a vocal-formant lead over the Rhodes, slowed and unhurried (a man with nowhere to be, not rushing), soft sub bass, brushed snare

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + layered warm pads, gated snare on 2 and 4 soft, a mellow saw doubling the lament gently, steam rising over a single bowl in the deepest still hour

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: lead drops, Rhodes center, an FM bell shadow, rain still visible through the steamed-up window

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: full but soft mix, the lament octave-doubled, weary but at rest

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on A (V of Dm), Rhodes sustains, the counter quiet — out to the river bridge next

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir downtempo 88 BPM D minor, warm mellow Rhodes, a weary five-note vocal-formant lead phrased like a slow unhurried sigh landing on the minor third, soft sub bass, brushed gated drums, layered warm pads, the low hum and pot-hiss of a tiny standing ramen counter, amber lamp warmth, rain on a steamed-up window, eating alone at 3am with nowhere to be, melancholic and intimate, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, the close interior of a tiny standing ramen counter under a single warm amber pendant lamp, steam rising from one bowl on a worn wooden counter, a lone figure seated alone, cyan neon glowing through a rain-streaked steamed-up window behind, warm amber against cold blue, cinematic shallow focus, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Am to Dm — the natural minor giving the warm, weary interior color of the ramen counter, the deepest still pause of the early-morning wander.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM, the album's first dip, the night slowing into its quietest interior — the wander pauses to rest and eat.
tracks.composer_notes: BRIDGE / re-introduces the SALARYMAN LAMENT (Vol. 1's motif #3) over a Rhodes — the same weary five-note sigh as Vol. 1, but SLOWED and unhurried: Vol. 1's lament was a man rushing and missing; Vol. 2's is a man with nowhere to be and all night to feel it. First tempo dip, the deepest interior pause. The D-minor / Rhodes pairing deliberately echoes Vol. 1's track-4 'Counter Seat, Whisky Neat' interior — a sibling refuge. Outro A (V of Dm) pivots to Gm of track 7 (the river bridge).

tracks.title: Bridge Over Black Water

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: sustained pad on G with the Dorian E natural passing color, the RAIN CELL stretched to slow half-time pads over the black river, single FM bell pings spaced wide, no rhythm, the city's reflection trembling in the water

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a half-tempo kick on 1 and 3, brushed snare on 4, deep sub bass, the rain cell at its most ambient and reflective, a mellow saw on long sustained notes

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + layered cold pads at the front of the mix, soft sidechain, the saw lead floating, the vast lonely view from a deserted river bridge in the deepest hour

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: lead drops, sustained pad, the rain cell pings clustering, the neon skyline mirrored and rippling below

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on D (V of Gm), pad sustains, the rain cell once more — toward the capsule hotel next

tracks.musicgen_prompt: cyberpunk noir ambient downtempo 84 BPM G minor Dorian with raised sixth E natural, a slow wet detuned FM bell rain figure stretched into slow half-time pads, half-tempo kick on one and three, brushed snare on beat four, a mellow saw lead floating on long sustained notes, layered cold pads, deep sub bass, distant rain, the vast lonely reflective stillness of a deserted river bridge in the deepest hour with the neon skyline trembling in black water, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, a lone figure standing at the railing of a deserted river bridge in the deepest hour of night, the magenta-and-cyan neon skyline of the city reflected and rippling in still black water below, fine rain, vast lonely reflective stillness, cinematic anamorphic, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Dm to Gm, voiced Dorian (raised 6th, E natural) — the harmony opens vast and reflective for the river bridge, the wander at its most suspended and spacious.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM, the slowest stretch of the deep night, the wander suspended into stillness over the water.
tracks.composer_notes: RAIN CELL stretched to half-time pads at its most ambient — the reflective center of the album, the river bridge in the deepest hour. Keep internal motion slow and spacious; never four-on-the-floor (the half-tempo kick is the most the kit ever does here). This is the structural still-point of the wander. Outro D (V of Gm) pivots to Cm of track 8 (the capsule hotel).

tracks.title: No Vacancy

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: pad in C minor in a tight low-ceilinged corridor reverb, soft kick on 1 and 3, the hum of a vending machine and an air-handler, cold blue capsule-hotel ambience

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: + a settled plucked arpeggio, warm bass, gated snare, the tired drift of being turned away at the desk, no capsule free

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: + a mellow saw lead, layered cold pads, soft sidechain, the cramped blue-lit corridor of a full capsule hotel, nowhere to lie down

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: lead drops, the arpeggio center, an FM bell shadow, the fatigue settling deeper

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: full but soft mix

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: filter close on G (V of Cm), pad sustains — back out into the lowest hour next

tracks.title: The Lowest Hour

tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fourth from Cm to Fm, voiced Phrygian for the album's darkest, most uncanny harmony — the flat-two (Gb) is the mark of the lowest, strangest hour, exactly the modal-darkening device Vol. 1 reserved for its neon core, here re-purposed for the emptiest moment of the stranded night.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -4 BPM, the album's floor reached at the lowest hour (82) — the EMOTIONAL peak comes not at high tempo but at the slowest, stillest, loneliest point; this is the album's anti-peak, its inverse of Vol. 1's 112-BPM dash.
tracks.composer_notes: PEAK (emotional, not kinetic). This is the album's structural climax — but as STILLNESS, not motion: the lowest hour, the emptiest and strangest moment, at the slowest tempo (82). The SALARYMAN LAMENT returns at its most fragmented, breaking apart over an F Phrygian floor (the b2 / Gb is the album's darkest harmonic color — Vol. 1 used Phrygian for its neon core; Vol. 2 uses it for the loneliest hour). This is the deliberate INVERSE of Vol. 1's track-7 peak ('Run For It', 112 BPM frantic dash): Vol. 2's peak is the quietest, slowest, most still track on the album. FLAG: the renderer must NOT speed this up or add a hard kit — the peak is emptiness and near-silence; if MusicGen drives it, the album's whole stillness thesis breaks. Outro C (V of Fm) pivots to Bbm of track 10 (the first turn toward dawn).

Keep it anticipatory and warming, not euphoric — the full resolution is the closer. Outro A# (V of D#m) pivots to C#m of track 12, closing the ring back to the home tonic at first light.

tracks.title: First Train Home

tracks.cover_prompt: no text, no words, no lettering, no faces visible. cyberpunk noir album cover, the first train pulling in at first light on a clean rain-washed platform, warm dawn light flooding in from the open end of the station, the neon now soft and fading against a clear brightening sky, a lone figure boarding the bright first train, the long night over, resolved warm hopeful mood, the first genuine warmth and light, cinematic anamorphic, no text, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from D#m to C#m, closing the ring back to the C# minor home tonic that opened Vol. 2 (and opened and closed Vol. 1) — but resolving with a Picardy-tinted major third in the final chord, the warmth Vol. 1 never allowed itself, earned by surviving the whole stranded night.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -8 BPM, settling to a gentle warm 84 for the close — not the album's slowest (that was the 82 lowest hour), because dawn brings a small steady forward warmth, the first train moving gently home.
tracks.composer_notes: CLOSER / RESOLUTION. This is the track the whole two-album arc was built toward: the platform motif resolved at first light. ALL FOUR Vol. 1 motifs resolve here together — the RAIN CELL returns whole and DRY for the first time in two albums (storm over); the LAST/FIRST-TRAIN PULSE is full and clean (the pulse that stranded him now carries him home); the SALARYMAN LAMENT plays whole but its final note LIFTS to the major third (a Picardy turn — the sigh that always landed on the minor third in Vol. 1 finally resolved); and the PLATFORM CHIME is restored to true pitch and, for the first time across two albums, sounds EARNED rather than ironic, because now there really is a train and a way home. The final chord glows with a Picardy major third — the album's only moment of true warmth and light, deliberately withheld until now (Vol. 1 'never resolved to comfort'; Vol. 2 earns it). The ring closes on the same C# minor that framed both albums, but where Vol. 1 ended on stillness UNRESOLVED, Vol. 2 ends on stillness RESOLVED. FLAG: the renderer must NOT add a fade-up tail or stinger and must NOT darken the Picardy ending — the warm resolved close into the morning IS the composition and the payoff of two albums; let the filter open into warmth, not close into darkness.

album_notes.overall_form: Stranding -> First Wander -> All-Night Counters -> Deepest Hour -> Turn Toward Dawn -> First Light Resolution over 12 tracks — a one-night STILLNESS-AND-WANDERING arc that is the deliberate INVERSE of Vol. 1's motion-and-chase. Where Vol. 1 ('Last Train') raced the clock to catch the last train and ended walking home in stillness unresolved, Vol. 2 ('After Hours') opens at the moment after the train is missed and ends catching the FIRST train home at dawn, stillness resolved. The shape is a slow descent into the lowest hour and a slow climb back into the light, framed by the same C# minor that opened and closed Vol. 1. Movements: STRANDING & FIRST WANDER (tracks 1-3): the shut gate and the last-train pulse silenced (1, C#m, slowest opener at 80), the locked echoing concourse and the realization of being stranded until dawn (2, F#m), and the step out into the soaked back-streets to wait it out (3, Bm Dorian, the rain cell spread into the puddles). ALL-NIGHT COUNTERS (tracks 4-6): the warm konbini refuge (4, Em, Rhodes), the uncanny still-open arcade with the platform chime warped (5, Am Dorian, the BPM ceiling at 92), and the standing ramen counter with the salaryman lament re-voiced weary-but-at-rest (6, Dm, Rhodes, first tempo dip). DEEPEST HOUR (tracks 7-9): the reflective river bridge with the rain cell stretched to half-time pads (7, Gm Dorian, the spacious still-point), the tired turned-away capsule-hotel corridor (8, Cm), and the EMOTIONAL PEAK as STILLNESS — the lowest, emptiest, strangest hour at the album's floor tempo (9, Fm Phrygian, 82 BPM, the lament fragmented, the deliberate inverse of Vol. 1's 112-BPM dash peak). TURN TOWARD DAWN (tracks 10-11): the sky going slate and the rain thinning as the first delivery trucks stir (10, Bbm, the rain cell thinning, the palette warming), and the station gate unlocking into grey-blue pre-dawn with the first-train pulse approaching and the platform chime half-restored (11, D#m Dorian). FIRST LIGHT RESOLUTION (track 12, C#m): the first train arrives and all four Vol. 1 motifs resolve together — the rain cell whole and dry, the first-train pulse clean, the salaryman lament lifting to a Picardy major third, the platform chime restored and earned — the ring closing on a C#-minor home that glows with a Picardy major third, the only true warmth in two albums. Tracks never cross 92 BPM: this is a pedestrian album, stillness and wandering, not a drive.
album_notes.key_strategy: A modal wander framed by the C# minor home tonic of Vol. 1, using Vol. 1's harmonic language (cycle-of-fifths and -fourths moves inside the minor mode, Dorian for bittersweet-warmth, Phrygian for the darkest hour) but routed for stillness rather than motion. C#m (the shut gate, picking up Vol. 1's home tonic) -> F#m (up a 5th, the concourse) -> Bm Dorian (up a 4th, the back-streets, bittersweet) -> Em (up a 4th, the warm konbini) -> Am Dorian (down a 4th, the uncanny arcade) -> Dm (down a 4th, the weary ramen counter) -> Gm Dorian (down a 4th, the reflective bridge) -> Cm (up a 4th, the cramped corridor) -> Fm Phrygian (down a 4th, the lowest hour, the b2 floor) -> Bbm (up a 5th, the turn toward dawn) -> D#m Dorian (up a 4th, the hopeful pre-dawn gate) -> C#m (up a 5th, closing the ring home, resolving with a Picardy major third). The mode shifts track the narrative: Dorian (raised 6th) marks the bittersweet-warm and hopeful moments (the back-streets, the arcade, the bridge, the pre-dawn gate); natural Aeolian marks the plainer interiors (concourse, konbini, ramen counter, corridor); Phrygian (b2) marks the single darkest, most uncanny hour (track 9). The one move Vol. 1 never made: the closer resolves to a Picardy-tinted C# MAJOR third in the final chord — the warmth Vol. 1 explicitly withheld ('the night never resolves to comfort'), here earned by surviving the whole stranded night. Versus Vol. 1's strict cycle-of-fifths descent-into-the-core-then-retrace-home ring (which never left minor), Vol. 2 is a looser modal wander that earns its one moment of major-mode resolution at first light.
album_notes.tempo_strategy: A strictly PEDESTRIAN band, 80-92 BPM — the album's hard cap is 92, far under Vol. 1's 112 peak (this is stillness and wandering, never the dash). The arc is a gentle settle into the lowest hour and a gentle climb back toward dawn, not a ramp: 80 (the stranded opener, the floor of the early tracks) -> 84 -> 88 -> 90 -> 92 (the all-night arcade, the busiest-with-noise but not fastest point) -> 88 (the ramen counter, first dip) -> 84 (the reflective bridge) -> 86 (the corridor) -> 82 (THE LOWEST HOUR — the album's tempo floor at the emotional peak; the peak is the SLOWEST, stillest track, the deliberate inverse of Vol. 1's 112-BPM dash peak) -> 88 -> 92 (the pre-dawn gate, anticipation) -> 84 (the warm first-light close). Every BPM sits in 80-92, comfortably inside the 80-104 pedestrian cap and the 80-118 validation range. The crucial design point: the album's structural climax (track 9) is its SLOWEST and stillest, not its fastest — emotion carried by emptiness and harmony, not speed; the audience is coding at 2am, and this album is the quiet hours after the last train, not the chase for it.
album_notes.motif_strategy: Four motifs, ALL CARRIED FORWARD from Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 and re-voiced/resolved for the stranded after-hours (no new primary motifs — Vol. 2's job is to answer Vol. 1's). (1) RAIN CELL (Vol. 1 #1, the descending four-note FM-bell weather signature) — re-voiced slower and wetter for the stillness, the album's weather thread: states the rain that strands the figure (1), spreads across the soaked back-streets into the puddles (3), stretches to half-time pads over the black river at its most ambient (7), THINS toward dawn as the storm breaks (10), and returns WHOLE AND DRY one last time at first light (12, the contour clean and undetuned for the first time in two albums — the weather signature at rest). (2) LAST-TRAIN PULSE -> FIRST-TRAIN PULSE (Vol. 1 #2, the square-wave countdown clatter, INVERTED) — the album's structural spine: opens as the last-train pulse RECEDING then SILENCED (the train missed, track 1), is absent through the long stranded hours (no train to count down), and RETURNS as the FIRST-train pulse approaching and warming at the pre-dawn gate (11) and full-and-clean as the first train arrives at first light (12); the pulse that meant 'about to be stranded' in Vol. 1 becomes the pulse that means 'about to go home'. (3) SALARYMAN LAMENT (Vol. 1 #3, the weary five-note sigh landing on the minor third) — re-voiced for exhaustion-without-urgency: stated solo and slowed at the ramen counter (6), fragmented at its loneliest over the Phrygian lowest hour (9), and played WHOLE one last time at first light with its final note LIFTING to the major third in a Picardy turn (12) — the resolution Vol. 1 never gave it (Vol. 1's lament walked home unaccompanied and unresolved; Vol. 2's reaches the morning). (4) PLATFORM CHIME (Vol. 1 #4, the too-bright two-note departure jingle) — the album's resolution thread and the 'platform motif returns resolved at first light' the album is built around: returns WARPED and drowned in the 3am arcade (5, a jingle for a station with no departures), HALF-RESTORED toward true pitch at the pre-dawn gate (11), and RESTORED fully to true pitch at first light (12) where for the first time across two albums it sounds EARNED rather than ironic, because now there really is a train and a way home. The whole album is the answer to Vol. 1: every motif that haunted the chase is resolved in the stillness of the morning after.
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Each track ends on a pitch that primes the next track's tonic, mostly via Vol. 1's cycle-of-fifths/fourths-inside-the-minor language, so transitions read as inevitable rather than abrupt. Specifically: 1 ends leaning toward F# minor (the figure leaves the platform). 2 ends on C# (V of F#m, rerooted as track 3's... actually C# is V of F#, and track 3 is Bm — track 2 closes on C# which functions as the dominant region pivoting by step down to Bm). 3 ends on F# (V of Bm, rerooted toward track 4's Em region). 4 ends on B (V of Em, pivoting to track 5's Am). 5 ends on E (V of Am, rerooted as track 6's Dm region). 6 ends on A (V of Dm, pivoting to track 7's Gm). 7 ends on D (V of Gm, rerooted toward track 8's Cm). 8 ends on G (V of Cm, pivoting to track 9's Fm). 9 ends on C (V of Fm, rerooted toward track 10's Bbm). 10 ends on F (V of Bbm, pivoting toward track 11's D#m). 11 ends on A# (V of D#m, rerooted as track 12's C#m, A# being the leading-tone/dominant region resolving home). 12 closes the ring on C# with a Picardy major third — a RESOLVED home cadence, the warmth Vol. 1 withheld. The listener won't consciously hear the modal wander — they will feel the night as a single continuous drift from the missed train to the first train, the lowest hour as a real bottom, and the first-light resolution as earned. Sections are crossfade-friendly so the album plays as one continuous stranded night.
album_notes.vol_continuity_notes: Vol. 2 is the DIRECT SEQUEL to Tokyo Cyberpunk Vol. 1 ('Last Train', slug tokyo-cyberpunk-vol-1) and is built as its mirror-answer. Vol. 1 was MOTION (a salaryman racing the clock through neon Shinjuku to catch the last train, peaking on a 112-BPM dash, ending walking home in stillness unresolved on C# minor at 80 BPM); Vol. 2 is STILLNESS (the same salaryman, having MISSED the last train, stranded until first service, wandering the after-hours city, ending catching the first train home at dawn in stillness RESOLVED on a Picardy-tinted C# at 84 BPM). Track 1 ('Missed It by a Minute') picks up exactly where Vol. 1 'The Long Way Home' left off — the same C#-minor home tonic, the empty platform, the rain — but the warmth of going home is gone: the LAST-TRAIN PULSE is heard once receding then silenced (the train left without him). All four Vol. 1 leitmotifs (Rain cell, Last-train pulse, Salaryman lament, Platform chime) are carried forward and RESOLVED at first light in track 12 — Vol. 2's entire purpose is to answer the motifs Vol. 1 left open. KEY/TEMPO LANGUAGE HONORED FROM VOL. 1: the same C#-minor home tonic framing the album; the same cycle-of-fifths/fourths-inside-the-minor harmonic moves; the same Dorian-for-bittersweet and Phrygian-for-the-dark modal vocabulary; the same +2/+4-BPM gentle-increment philosophy. The crucial DEPARTURE: Vol. 1 capped tempo at 112 (the dash) and never resolved to major comfort; Vol. 2 caps tempo at 92 (strictly pedestrian — no dash exists in this album) and earns a single Picardy-major resolution at first light. This is also DISTINCT from neo-tokyo-drive (the slow pedestrian sub-104 neon-city album): Vol. 2 shares the pedestrian tempo philosophy but is specifically the STRANDED-AFTER-HOURS night (missed-train-to-first-train) with the carried Vol. 1 motifs and the C#-minor sequel frame, not a generic slow neon cruise.
album_notes.visual_continuity_notes: Twelve covers narrate the stranding -> wander -> all-night-counters -> deepest-hour -> dawn-turn -> first-light journey within Vol. 1's established neon-noir visual family: the consistent magenta/cyan neon-and-rain palette, anamorphic cinematic framing, deep wet-asphalt reflections, the lone salaryman protagonist (always from behind or distant, no faces), and Vol. 1's no-warm-sunset rule held UNTIL the final two tracks. The Vol. 2-specific arc is a SLOW PALETTE WARMING reserved for the dawn: tracks 1-9 stay in Vol. 1's cold magenta/cyan neon-and-rain (the shut gate, the empty concourse, the soaked back-streets, the konbini's cold-warm fluorescents, the uncanny arcade, the steam-amber ramen counter, the black-water bridge, the blue capsule corridor, the empty 4am crossing); track 10 introduces the first grey-slate daylight competing with the neon as the rain thins; track 11 brings grey-blue pre-dawn to the platform; and track 12 is the album's only WARM cover — the first clean dawn light flooding the rain-washed platform as the first train pulls in, the neon now soft against a brightening sky (the visual payoff of two albums, the warmth Vol. 1 never showed). Covers 4 and 6 carry small warm interior pools (the konbini, the ramen counter) as Vol. 1's track-4 'Counter Seat' did — sibling refuges. Per the future-albums native-aspect-gen rule, generate covers at native aspect buckets (1024 square + 1344x768 + 768x1344) from scratch rather than outpainting. Every cover_prompt front-loads text and face suppression and ends with 'no text, 1024x1024'.
album_notes.execution_notes_for_pipeline: MusicGen / ACE-Step is the renderer of record (license risk accepted). Every musicgen_prompt is explicit about 'instrumental' and 'no vocals'; the 'vocal-formant lead' references (the salaryman lament) are an INSTRUMENTAL synth lead voiced to evoke a vocal sigh — they carry NO lyrics, NO words, NO phonemes (same as Vol. 1's lament); do not relax the no-vocals directive in any per-track override. Per-track durations sum to 2970 seconds (49.5 minutes) for the 12 — comfortably over the 45-minute album floor. Long tracks: 1 (252s, the stranded opener needs room to set the after-hours stillness and pick up the Vol. 1 seam), 6 (252s, the ramen-counter lament re-voicing), 7 (264s, the reflective river-bridge still-point), 9 (270s, the lowest-hour emotional peak — longest, the album's structural climax-as-stillness), 12 (288s, the first-light resolution where all four motifs resolve — the payoff of two albums). The rest sit at 234-240s. ALL BPMs sit in 80-92 — strictly inside the pedestrian cap (<=104) and the 80-118 validation range (album range 80-92). CRITICAL RENDER FLAGS: (a) track 9 ('The Lowest Hour') must NOT be sped up or given a hard kit by the renderer — the emotional peak is near-silence and stillness at the slowest tempo; if MusicGen drives it, the album's whole stillness thesis breaks; (b) track 12 ('First Train Home') must NOT get a fade-up tail or stinger and must NOT be darkened — the warm resolved Picardy close into the morning IS the composition and the two-album payoff; let the filter open into warmth. WEATHER ROUTING: theme is Tokyo/Japan — slug 'tokyo-cyberpunk-vol-2' should route to the JAPAN region in nightdrive-encoder::weather (Tokyo and Japanese cities, per the per-album-weather system and the 'TWC cities must match album theme' rule; Vol. 1 'Last Train' was the first album on the JP weather system — Vol. 2 inherits the same JP routing). Verify 'tokyo'/'japan' keyword routing exists in region_for; if it routes only on a vol-1-specific slug, ensure 'tokyo-cyberpunk' substring matching covers vol-2. Per the sync-drop rule for future albums, target a 1-shot synchronized public flip at one anchor timestamp (run-album --publish-at). Mind the YouTube caps for a 12-track album (the ~10/day channel rolling-24h cap, the 6/day GCP per-project video.insert cap, Pacific-midnight resets) — stagger the uploads across a reset or use publish-staggered 6/day and set the sync-drop anchor accordingly.
album_notes.title_safety_notes: danger_zone_keys = ['tokyo_cyberpunk', 'akira', 'blade_runner']. Per the album_title_danger_zone rule, a title is rejected only on a DOUBLE-HIT: BOTH (a) a named element of Akira or Blade Runner AND (b) a track title on those films' canonical soundtracks (Geinoh Yamashirogumi's 'Akira' / 'Symphonic Suite Akira', or Vangelis's 'Blade Runner' soundtrack). All twelve Vol. 2 titles are GENERIC after-hours-noir scene descriptors and were checked clear: 'Missed It by a Minute', 'Locked Concourse', 'Back-Streets, No Umbrella', 'Konbini, 2 a.m.', 'Arcade Still Open', 'One Bowl, Steam Rising', 'Bridge Over Black Water', 'No Vacancy', 'The Lowest Hour', 'First Delivery Trucks', 'Gate Unlocks', 'First Train Home' — none names a specific character, vehicle, location, or line of dialogue from Akira or Blade Runner, and none matches a track title on the Geinoh Yamashirogumi Akira soundtrack (e.g. 'Kaneda', 'Tetsuo', 'Battle Against Clown', 'Requiem', 'Mutation', 'Exodus from the Underground Fortress', 'Illusion', 'Shomyo', 'Doll's Polyphony') or the Vangelis Blade Runner soundtrack (e.g. 'Main Titles', 'Blush Response', 'Wait for Me', 'Rachel's Song', 'Love Theme', 'One More Kiss Dear', 'Blade Runner Blues', 'Memories of Green', 'Tales of the Future', 'Damask Rose', 'Tears in Rain'). DANGER-ZONE TITLE AVOIDED: 'Tears in the Rain' / 'Tears in Rain' was considered for the rain-soaked closer or the river-bridge track and REJECTED — it is BOTH the famous Blade Runner 'tears in rain' monologue (a named/quoted element of the film) AND 'Tears in Rain' is a track on Vangelis's canonical Blade Runner soundtrack: a double-hit, takedown territory. It was replaced with the generic 'Bridge Over Black Water' (track 7) and 'First Train Home' (track 12). 'Memories of Green' (a possible nostalgic-track title) was similarly avoided as it is a Vangelis Blade Runner soundtrack track. 'Konbini' and 'salaryman' are generic Japanese-life nouns, not film-specific, and are safe.
